figures.gloss: The ultimate, unmanifest state of the Divine before creation, consisting of Ain (Nothingness), Ain Soph (Limitlessness), and Ain Soph Aur (Limitless Light). It is the negative veil of existence from which the ten Sephiroth of the Tree of Life sequentially emanate.
figures.facet: Unlike the Jewish Ein Sof which remains strictly monotheistic and abstract, Hermeticism treats this as a Neoplatonic source-code that can be mathematically mapped, meditated upon, and magically accessed.
figures.provenance: Isaac Luria (via Knorr von Rosenroth's 'Kabbala Denudata', 1677); MacGregor Mathers.

figures.gloss: The central glyph of Western occultism, comprising ten Sephiroth (divine emanations) and twenty-two connecting paths. It serves as a universal map of macrocosmic creation and the microcosmic human soul, organizing all planetary, elemental, and divine forces into a single geometric diagram.
figures.facet: In Hermeticism, it is divorced from strict Jewish theology and transformed into a universal filing system where Egyptian gods, Tarot cards, and alchemical metals are assigned to each node.
figures.provenance: Athanasius Kircher, 'Oedipus Aegyptiacus' (1652); Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn.

figures.gloss: The primordial, macrocosmic human being whose metaphysical 'body' is composed of the ten Sephiroth. He is the first emanation of the Divine, the perfect blueprint of the universe, and the ultimate spiritual state that the adept seeks to reconstruct.
figures.facet: Hermeticists literalize Adam Kadmon as the 'Grand Man' of the cosmos, making him the ultimate target of spiritual alchemy: the magician's goal is to become this perfected, god-like anthropos.
figures.provenance: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, 'Three Books of Occult Philosophy' (1531).

figures.gloss: The four-letter name of God (YHVH), representing the four classical elements. Renaissance occultists transformed it by inserting the Hebrew letter Shin (Spirit) to form YHSVH (Yeheshuah), unifying the blind elemental forces under the conscious direction of divine spirit.
figures.facet: A specific Renaissance invention designed to map Christian theology onto Jewish mysticism, effectively turning the name of Jesus into the supreme magical formula for elemental mastery and psychic integration.
figures.provenance: Johann Reuchlin, 'De Arte Cabalistica' (1517); Athanasius Kircher.

figures.gloss: The syncretic god of wisdom, magic, and writing, typically assigned to the Sephirah Hod (Glory) and the eighth path of the Tree. He is the revealer of the Qabalistic mysteries, the master of the Tarot, and the archetypal patron of the magus.
figures.facet: This figure fully merges the Egyptian scribe of the gods with Hellenistic mysticism and Qabalistic planetary correspondences, serving as the active, initiating intelligence of Western ceremonial magic.
figures.provenance: 'Corpus Hermeticum' (trans. Marsilio Ficino, 1471); Aleister Crowley, 'The Book of Thoth' (1944).

figures.gloss: The twenty-two Major Arcana cards, paired with the twenty-two Hebrew letters to constitute the paths between the Sephiroth. Magicians use these archetypal images for 'pathworking', projecting their consciousness into the cards to ascend the Tree of Life experientially.
figures.facet: Hermetic Qabalah explicitly retrofitted the medieval Italian playing-card deck into the supreme mystical key of the universe, turning a parlor game into a profound engine of astral initiation.
figures.provenance: Eliphas Levi, 'Dogme et Rituel de la Haute Magie' (1854); Arthur Edward Waite.

figures.gloss: The third Sephirah, Understanding. She is the Supernal Mother, the dark, restrictive womb of form, associated with the planet Saturn, time, and the great primordial ocean (Marah) from which all structural life and limitation emerge into the lower universe.
figures.facet: Hermeticism highly syncretizes this abstract Sephirah with pagan goddesses like Hekate, Isis, and Babalon, blending Jewish mystical architecture with the pagan Divine Feminine and the sorrow of material incarnation.

figures.gloss: The unbalanced, destructive shells or husks left over from a previous, failed creation. They form an inverted, demonic Tree of Death beneath the Tree of Life, populated by archdemons, chaotic forces, and the fragmented remnants of unbalanced severity.
figures.facet: While traditional Kabbalah views them merely as husks of impurity to be avoided, Hermeticism often treats them as an active, dark mirror-universe—the 'Nightside'—that the adept must eventually conquer or integrate.
figures.provenance: Isaac Luria; Kenneth Grant, 'Nightside of Eden' (1977).

figures.gloss: The first Sephirah and the Crown of the Tree, the point of pure, undifferentiated unity where the Limitless Light first contracts into a knowable point of existence. It carries the divine name Eheieh ('I am that I am'), the archangel Metatron, and the Primum Mobile; every path of ascent on the Tree terminates here.
figures.facet: Kether is the Tree's alpha and omega at once — Golden Dawn doctrine holds that 'Kether is in Malkuth, and Malkuth is in Kether, but after another manner,' making the Crown not a distant summit but a point folded into the base of the whole structure.
figures.provenance: Sefer Yetzirah; Athanasius Kircher, 'Oedipus Aegyptiacus' (1652); S.L. MacGregor Mathers, 'The Kabbalah Unveiled' (1887).

figures.gloss: The sixth Sephirah, Beauty, the solar heart at the center of the Tree where the opposing pillars of Severity and Mercy balance. It is assigned the sun, sacrificed-and-resurrected god-forms (Christ, Osiris, Bacchus, the Slain God generally), and the child, and is the seat of the adept's attainment of Knowledge and Conversation of the Holy Guardian Angel.
figures.facet: Hermetic Qabalah's most doctrinally distinctive move: where Jewish Kabbalah reads Tiphareth simply as harmonizing Beauty, the Golden Dawn and its heirs stack every dying-and-rising god of the Mediterranean onto this single node, making the Tree's center a syncretic solar-sacrifice engine.
figures.provenance: S.L. MacGregor Mathers and the Golden Dawn's grade-and-Sephirah correspondences; Aleister Crowley, '777' (1909) and 'The Book of Thoth' (1944); Israel Regardie, 'The Golden Dawn' (1937-40).

figures.gloss: The tenth and final Sephirah, the Kingdom — the material world, the physical body, and the 'Bride' in exile from her royal household above. It is the vessel into which all nine higher emanations finally descend, and the sole Sephirah the embodied adept starts from.
figures.facet: In Kabbalah proper, Malkuth is explicitly identified with the Shekhinah, God's indwelling presence exiled within matter; Hermetic Qabalah inherits this as the doctrine that redemption is the raising of the fallen, material Bride back to reunion with the Crown — the Great Work read as a marriage restored.
figures.provenance: The Zohar; Dion Fortune, 'The Mystical Qabalah' (1935); Israel Regardie, 'The Golden Dawn'.

figures.gloss: The Abyss and its bridging non-Sephirah, 'Knowledge' — the gulf between the supernal triad (Kether, Chokmah, Binah) and the seven lower Sephiroth. Da'ath has no fixed place among the ten emanations and is variously described as an eleventh, false, or reflected Sephirah, tenanted by Choronzon, the demon of dispersion.
figures.facet: Crossing the Abyss is Hermetic Qabalah's supreme ordeal, not a Jewish Kabbalistic one: Crowley's Grade of Master of the Temple is defined by the ego's annihilation at exactly this gulf, and Kenneth Grant's later 'Typhonian' current makes Da'ath the very gateway into the Qliphothic Nightside.
figures.provenance: Aleister Crowley, 'The Vision and the Voice' (1911) and 'Magick in Theory and Practice' (1929); Kenneth Grant, 'Nightside of Eden' (1977).

figures.gloss: The practice of computing the numerical value of Hebrew (and by extension English or Greek) letters to reveal hidden equivalences between words and divine names — the working method by which the Tree's correspondences are cross-checked and expanded.
figures.facet: Gematria is the Tree's engine room rather than its architecture: it is how a practitioner justifies that one word 'is' another, turning the static filing-system of the Sephiroth into a live, generative combinatorics of meaning.
figures.provenance: Sefer Yetzirah; Aleister Crowley, '777 and Other Qabalistic Writings' (1909, incl. 'Sepher Sephiroth').

figures.gloss: The fourfold division of all reality into Atziluth (Emanation), Briah (Creation), Yetzirah (Formation) and Assiah (Action) — each a complete Tree of Life in itself, keyed to the four letters of the Tetragrammaton (Yod-Heh-Vav-Heh) and the four Tarot suits (Wands, Cups, Swords, Pentacles).
figures.facet: The Four Worlds let Hermetic Qabalah nest the Tree inside itself four times over, so that a single Sephirah such as Tiphareth names four distinct grades of reality at once — the system's answer to how one map can hold both the purely spiritual and the frankly physical.
figures.provenance: Kabbala Denudata (Knorr von Rosenroth, 1677-84); S.L. MacGregor Mathers, 'The Kabbalah Unveiled'; Israel Regardie, 'The Golden Dawn'.

figures.gloss: Pen name of Alphonse-Louis Constant (1810-1875), the French ex-seminarian magus whose 'Dogme et Rituel de la Haute Magie' (1854-56) first published the correspondence of the 22 Tarot trumps to the 22 Hebrew letters and paths of the Tree — the single synthesis every later Hermetic Qabalah school (Golden Dawn, Thelema, B.O.T.A.) inherited wholesale.
figures.facet: Levi worked from no ancient source for the Tarot-Hebrew correspondence — it is his own 19th-century invention, presented with such confident erudition that it was received as recovered ancient wisdom rather than personal synthesis, becoming self-fulfilling doctrine.
figures.provenance: Eliphas Levi, 'Dogme et Rituel de la Haute Magie' (1854-56); A.E. Waite's English translation, 'Transcendental Magic' (1896).

figures.gloss: Pen name of Violet Mary Firth (1890-1946), initiated in an Alpha et Omega temple (a Golden Dawn successor order) before founding the Fraternity (later Society) of the Inner Light in 1922. Her 'The Mystical Qabalah' (1935) is the tradition's most widely read modern textbook, translating the Tree of Life's correspondences into the language of Jungian-inflected psychology.
figures.facet: Fortune reframes the Sephiroth as stages of psychological integration rather than only theurgic stations, making her the hinge figure between Golden Dawn ceremonialism and the therapeutic, self-development register in which most 20th- and 21st-century Qabalah is now taught.
figures.provenance: Dion Fortune, 'The Mystical Qabalah' (1935); Gareth Knight, 'Dion Fortune and the Inner Light' (2000).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The unmanifest limitless light and the structured cosmic geometric vessel into which it pours.
Intra-tradition: The Tree of Death acts as the shadow-reflection and dark, unbalanced inversion of the Tree of Life.
Intra-tradition: The foundational 19th-century synthesis mapping the 22 Tarot trumps onto the 22 pathways of the Tree.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Directly borrowed and adapted from Jewish mysticism, though Hermeticism strips it of its strict monotheistic boundaries.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, indivisible, and unmanifest source of all existence before the emanation of dualistic reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An impersonal, absolute, and limitless divine reality that transcends all attributes and forms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cosmic trees that serve as the structural framework of the universe, connecting divine, human, and underworld realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A central axis mundi that maps the flow of divine forces down into the material world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A structured map of divine emanations (Sephiroth or Aeons) that bridge the gap between the infinite and the material.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A geometric cosmogram charting the flow of spiritual energy, human incarnation, and the soul's initiatory journey.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The central figure of Hermeticism, explicitly identified with the Qabalistic pathways of magic and intellect.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Egyptian god of magic and writing, explicitly invoked by Hermeticists as the archetypal master of the Tarot and Qabalah.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial, macrocosmic anthropomorphic blueprint from whose metaphysical body the universe is constructed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A Renaissance Christian Kabbalistic adaptation of the Tetragrammaton, intended to embed Christ into the ultimate Jewish name of God.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The structural embodiment of cosmic imbalance, destruction, and the dark opposition to divine order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The divine feminine emanation of wisdom and understanding who gives birth to the structural forms of the lower universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Hermeticists actively syncretized the Qabalistic Sephirah of the Supernal Mother with the Egyptian goddess of magic and motherhood.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A recreational game format structurally transformed into a profound cosmological and divinatory map of the universe's mechanics.
Intra-tradition: Kether is the Tree's apex node, the first point the whole glyph's emanation begins from.
The Tree's own maxim: 'Kether is in Malkuth, and Malkuth is in Kether, but after another manner' — first and last Sephirah mirror each other as alpha and omega of the emanation.
Joined by the direct Path of Gimel (the High Priestess), the shortest route across the Abyss from Crown to solar heart.
Kether is the same first emanation of the Jewish Sefirot Hermeticism adopted wholesale, stripped of its strict theological restrictions.
Intra-tradition: the sixth Sephirah is the Tree's central, balancing node on the Middle Pillar.
the same sixth emanation of the Jewish Sefirot, onto which Hermeticism additionally stacks pagan dying-and-rising gods.
Golden Dawn doctrine assigns the attainment of the Holy Guardian Angel's Knowledge and Conversation specifically to Tiphareth, the Adeptus Minor grade's central goal.
Intra-tradition: the tenth Sephirah is the Tree's base, the final vessel every higher emanation descends into.
In Kabbalah, Malkuth is explicitly identified with the Shekhinah, the divine presence exiled within matter — Hermeticism inherits this equation as the 'Bride' awaiting redemption.
the same tenth and final emanation of the Jewish Sefirot.
Intra-tradition: the non-Sephirah gulf built onto the Tree between the supernal triad and the seven lower nodes.
Crossing the Abyss opens directly onto the Nightside; in Kenneth Grant's system Da'ath is itself the threshold into the Qliphothic realm.
Both are correspondence-engines of the same system: gematria computes word-identities by letter-number, the Tarot maps the same 22 Hebrew letters onto pictorial paths.
Gematria is the numerical method by which the Tree's correspondences (as tabulated in works like Crowley's '777') are computed and cross-referenced.
Each of the Four Worlds contains a complete Tree of Life; the fourfold division reads the same ten Sephiroth four times over, in Atziluth, Briah, Yetzirah and Assiah.
The Tetragrammaton's four letters are keyed one-for-one to the Four Worlds; the inserted Shin of the Pentagrammaton adds Spirit as a fifth, unifying element across all four.
Levi's 'Dogme et Rituel' (1854) performed the trumps-to-Hebrew-letters synthesis this figure describes, already named in its own provenance.
Levi's Tarot-Qabalah synthesis directly seeded the Golden Dawn's own Book of Thoth correspondences a generation later, a documented line of transmission.
Fortune's 'The Mystical Qabalah' (1935) is the cited provenance source for the Binah entry; she systematized this Sephirah's psychological framing for 20th-century practice.
Fortune trained in an Alpha et Omega temple (a Golden Dawn successor order) before writing her own Tree-of-Life textbook, carrying the Order's system forward.

Golden Dawn hermeticists adopted Lurianic Kabbalah's Adam Kadmon wholesale, converting the primordial configuration of divine light into a literal template of self-deification the magician works to embody.

Hermetic Qabalah adopted the Jewish term and structure wholesale as its 'Nightside': Kabbalah's Other Side is impurity to be quarantined and mended, the Hermetic Qliphoth a dark mirror-Tree to be explored, conquered, or integrated.

The same ten-emanation glyph Hermeticism adopted wholesale from Jewish Kabbalah (as already recorded node-by-node for Kether, Tiphareth, and Malkuth), converting a monotheist contemplative doctrine into a universal correspondence-filing system for pagan gods, metals, and Tarot.
The Golden Dawn built its operative initiation engine directly on Hermetic Qabalah's universalized Tree, pinning its grade structure onto the same ten Sephiroth.
Same 78-card deck: the divinatory arcana retrofitted wholesale by 19th-century occultists as the 32 Paths of Wisdom on the Qabalistic Tree of Life.

Identical retrofitted deck under the same 32-Paths Qabalistic scheme; the Golden Dawn version adds only its own proprietary attributions (Justice/Strength swap, secret Book T) atop the generic Hermetic Qabalah mapping.

dossier.essence: The Western esoteric adaptation of Jewish Kabbalah, developed from the Renaissance 'Christian Cabala' (Pico della Mirandola, Reuchlin, Agrippa, from c. 1486) into the occult system codified by 19th- and 20th-century magicians — Éliphas Lévi, the Golden Dawn, Aleister Crowley, Dion Fortune. It reworks the Kabbalistic Tree of Life into a universal map of cosmos and psyche onto which Tarot, astrology, alchemy, and the gods of many pantheons are correlated, and it remains the working framework of much Western occultism (conventionally spelled 'Qabalah' to mark this stream).
dossier.cosmos: All existence emanates from the unmanifest — the 'negative veils' of Ain, Ain Soph, and Ain Soph Aur (Nothingness, the Limitless, the Limitless Light) — down the ten Sephiroth and twenty-two paths of the Tree of Life, from Kether (Crown) to Malkuth (the material Kingdom). The Tree is at once the structure of the macrocosm and of the human soul, mirrored in the 'primordial man' Adam Kadmon; its shadow-side is the Qliphoth, the unbalanced 'shells,' a Tree of Death. The adept's work is to ascend the Tree, integrating each sphere, toward union with the divine.
dossier.practice: Chiefly a contemplative-magical discipline: meditation on the Tree and its tables of correspondence, 'pathworking' (guided visionary ascent of the paths, using the Tarot trumps as gates), ritual invocation keyed to the Sephiroth and the divine names, and the cultivation of the 'Holy Guardian Angel.' It has no congregation or ordained clergy; it is transmitted through initiatory orders (the Golden Dawn and its successors), through books, and through solitary study.
dossier.canon: No single scripture: it takes the Tree and the '32 Paths' from the Jewish 'Sefer Yetzirah' and the 'Zohar' (via Christian intermediaries such as Knorr von Rosenroth's 'Kabbala Denudata,' 1677) and is defined by later synthesizing works — Agrippa's 'Three Books of Occult Philosophy' (1533), Lévi's 'Dogme et rituel de la haute magie' (1856), and Dion Fortune's 'The Mystical Qabalah' (1935).
dossier.reflects: The syncretic, systematizing genius of Western esotericism — a borrowed mystical architecture turned into a universal filing-cabinet in which every symbol of every tradition can be assigned a drawer and worked as magic.
sources: Gershom Scholem, 'Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism' (1941)
Wouter J. Hanegraaff, 'Esotericism and the Academy: Rejected Knowledge in Western Culture' (2012)
Joseph Dan, 'Kabbalah: A Very Short Introduction' (2006)
Nicholas Goodrick-Clarke, 'The Western Esoteric Traditions: A Historical Introduction' (2008)
